stem:
A 49-year-old G3P3 presents with a 9-month history of heavy menstrual bleeding. Cycles have shortened from 28 to 22 days, last 8 to 10 days, and she changes a fully soaked pad every 90 minutes on the worst two days with passage of 5-cent-piece-sized clots. She reports two episodes of intermenstrual bleeding in the last 3 months. Her BMI is 34, she has type 2 diabetes on metformin, and she has never been on hormonal contraception.

There is no postcoital bleeding, no dyspareunia, and her last cervical screen 18 months ago was HPV negative. She is not sexually active currently and uses no contraception. On examination her abdomen is soft with no palpable mass; speculum shows a healthy cervix and no cervical lesion; bimanual reveals a bulky but mobile uterus, no adnexal mass. Pregnancy test is negative. FBC shows Hb 98 g/L with MCV 74 fL and ferritin 6 microg/L. TSH and coagulation screen are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/84 mmHg, HR 82 bpm, T 36.6 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: BMI 34. Bulky mobile uterus. Cervix healthy. No adnexal mass.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 98 g/L, MCV 74 fL, ferritin 6 microg/L, TSH normal, coags normal, HPV negative 18 months ago.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Commence tranexamic acid 1 g tds during menses and review
options.rationale: TXA is first-line medical therapy for HMB without structural cause, but empirical treatment in a woman >45 with IMB and unopposed-oestrogen risk factors without first excluding malignancy is inappropriate. TXA is correct after endometrial sampling excludes hyperplasia/cancer (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test with HPV partial genotyping today
options.rationale: She is in routine 5-yearly screening interval with recent negative HPV; bleeding pattern points to endometrial rather than cervical source. Repeat CST is correct with new cervical symptoms or visible cervical lesion (NCSP; RANZCOG).

options.text: Pelvic MRI to characterise the bulky uterus before further management
options.rationale: MRI is second-line imaging used to map fibroids prior to surgery or when TVUS is inconclusive; not first investigation. MRI is correct after TVUS identifies a complex finding requiring better characterisation (RANZCOG).

options.text: Transvaginal ultrasound and outpatient endometrial biopsy at this visit
options.rationale: Age over 45 with HMB plus intermenstrual bleeding and obesity/diabetes (unopposed oestrogen risk factors) mandates endometrial assessment to exclude hyperplasia/carcinoma; TVUS plus Pipelle biopsy is the recommended first-line workup (RANZCOG AUB statement; Therapeutic Guidelines Gynaecology).

options.text: Outpatient hysteroscopy without prior endometrial sampling or pelvic imaging
options.rationale: Hysteroscopy is indicated when TVUS shows focal pathology or when blind sampling is non-diagnostic; initial workup is TVUS plus Pipelle. Hysteroscopy-first is correct only when TVUS already shows focal lesion (e.g., polyp) (RANZCOG; NICE NG88).

explanation.summary: In HMB, age over 45, intermenstrual bleeding, persistent symptoms despite treatment, or risk factors for unopposed oestrogen exposure (obesity, diabetes, PCOS, tamoxifen, nulliparity, late menopause) require endometrial assessment before empirical medical therapy. TVUS measures endometrial thickness and identifies structural PALM lesions; Pipelle biopsy samples the endometrium for hyperplasia/carcinoma.
key_points: PALM (structural): Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia
COEIN (non-structural): Coagulopathy, Ovulatory, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified
Endometrial sampling indicated in HMB >45, IMB, failed medical Rx, or risk factors
Management ladder if benign: tranexamic acid, NSAIDs, LNG-IUS, COCP, oral progestogen, ablation, hysterectomy
LNG-IUS is first-line long-term medical therapy for HMB without structural cause
explanation.pearls: A bulky uterus on bimanual examination in a perimenopausal woman with IMB is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise, regardless of how plausible adenomyosis or fibroids seem.

subtopic_detail: Heavy menstrual bleeding - investigation in a perimenopausal woman

stem:
A 34-year-old woman presents with her fifth episode of vulvovaginal candidiasis in the past 12 months. Each episode features intense vulval pruritus, dyspareunia, thick white curd-like discharge, and erythematous vulval skin, and resolves with a single oral fluconazole 150 mg dose. Between episodes she is asymptomatic. She is in a stable monogamous relationship for 5 years, uses copper IUD for contraception, has no history of diabetes, and a recent HbA1c is 5.4% (35 mmol/mol).

She does not use scented soaps, wears cotton underwear, and has not used antibiotics in the last 6 months. She has no immunosuppression. Today on speculum examination there is thick white adherent discharge with vulval erythema and excoriation. Vaginal pH is 4.2. KOH microscopy shows numerous pseudohyphae and budding yeast. A high vaginal swab for fungal culture and speciation is collected and confirms Candida albicans sensitive to fluconazole.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/74 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, T 36.5 degC, BMI 24.
data_table.Examination: Thick white discharge, vulval erythema and excoriation.
data_table.Investigations: Vaginal pH 4.2. KOH: pseudohyphae and budding yeast. HVS culture: Candida albicans, fluconazole sensitive. HbA1c 5.4% (35 mmol/mol).

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate ongoing management?

options.text: Single dose fluconazole 150 mg orally and reassure this is an isolated acute episode
options.rationale: She has had at least 4 episodes in 12 months, meeting criteria for recurrent VVC; isolated single-dose treatment without induction and suppression has high relapse rate. Single-dose fluconazole is correct for sporadic acute episodes only (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

options.text: Induction fluconazole 150 mg orally days 1, 4, 7, then suppressive fluconazole 150 mg weekly for 6 months
options.rationale: Recurrent VVC (>=4 episodes/year) is managed with an induction regimen of fluconazole 150 mg every 72 hours for 3 doses to clear, followed by weekly suppressive fluconazole 150 mg for at least 6 months; this halves recurrence rates (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic; CDC STI Treatment 2021).

options.text: Empirical oral metronidazole 400 mg twice daily for a 7-day course without further investigation
options.rationale: Metronidazole treats bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis, not candidiasis; KOH confirms fungal infection. Metronidazole is correct in BV and trichomoniasis, never in confirmed candidiasis (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

options.text: Refer her for an oral glucose tolerance test before any further antifungal therapy
options.rationale: Diabetes is a risk factor but HbA1c 5.4% effectively excludes it; OGTT not warranted as a precondition for treatment. OGTT is correct only with HbA1c borderline or unexplained recurrent infections persisting after suppression (RACGP Red Book; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: Long-term intravaginal boric acid 600 mg nightly indefinitely as continuous suppressive antifungal therapy regimen
options.rationale: Intravaginal boric acid is reserved for non-albicans species (C. glabrata) or azole-resistant infection; her isolate is fluconazole-sensitive C. albicans. Boric acid is correct after speciation confirms non-albicans Candida (Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic).

explanation.summary: Recurrent VVC is defined as 4 or more symptomatic episodes per year, confirmed by microscopy or culture. Management combines an induction phase (fluconazole 150 mg orally on days 1, 4 and 7) with suppression (fluconazole 150 mg weekly for at least 6 months). Address modifiable factors: optimise glycaemic control, avoid douching and irritants, and consider non-albicans Candida if treatment-resistant. Liver function should be checked before prolonged azole therapy.
key_points: RVVC: >=4 culture-confirmed episodes per year
Induction: fluconazole 150 mg PO days 1, 4 and 7
Suppression: fluconazole 150 mg weekly for 6 months
Test for non-albicans Candida if resistant (glabrata responds to topical boric acid)
Screen for poorly controlled diabetes and consider HIV in immunocompromised picture
explanation.pearls: Routine OGTT is not needed when HbA1c is well below the diabetes threshold; the diagnostic role of HbA1c here is to exclude diabetes as the driver, not to gatekeep treatment.

stem:
A 55-year-old G3P3 presents with 18 months of urinary leakage. She leaks small volumes when she coughs, laughs, sneezes, or lifts her grandchildren, has no urgency, no nocturia, and no leakage on the way to the toilet. She empties her bladder 6 times a day, never wakes overnight to void, and does not feel incomplete emptying. There is no haematuria, no dysuria, and no faecal incontinence.

She had three vaginal births with two episiotomies and a forceps delivery for her second child. Her last period was 14 months ago. BMI 31. She smokes 10 cigarettes/day. On examination, abdomen soft, no palpable bladder. On speculum a first-degree anterior wall prolapse is noted. A cough stress test in semi-recumbent and standing positions reproduces leakage of urine from the urethral meatus. Urinalysis is clear and post-void residual on bladder scan is 35 mL.

data_table.Examination: First-degree anterior wall prolapse. Positive cough stress test. No pelvic mass.
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis clear. PVR 35 mL. Bladder diary not yet completed.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Refer for a mid-urethral sling (transobturator tape) surgical procedure now
options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for women who have failed adequate conservative therapy including supervised pelvic floor training and lifestyle modification; first-line is conservative. Surgery is correct only after >=3 months supervised PFMT with persistent symptoms (RANZCOG; Continence Foundation Australia).

options.text: Commence oral oxybutynin 5 mg twice daily as first-line monotherapy for symptoms
options.rationale: Oxybutynin is antimuscarinic indicated for urgency UI; history is pure stress incontinence so antimuscarinics will not help and may cause cognitive/anticholinergic side effects. Oxybutynin is correct in urgency-predominant UI (AMH).

options.text: Three-day bladder diary, pelvic floor training for 3 months, weight loss and smoking cessation
options.rationale: First-line for stress urinary incontinence is supervised pelvic floor muscle training for at least 3 months alongside weight loss (5 to 10% reduction halves leakage episodes) and smoking cessation (reduces cough-driven leakage); a bladder diary characterises voiding and is recommended before further intervention (Continence Foundation Australia; RANZCOG).

options.text: Insert a ring pessary for the management of stress urinary incontinence
options.rationale: Continence pessary may be useful for SUI but is second-line conservative after PFMT; first-degree prolapse is not the primary driver here. Pessary is correct as adjunct after failed PFMT or with prolapse contribution (RANZCOG).

options.text: Urodynamic studies as the first step before any conservative therapy is trialled
options.rationale: Urodynamics are not routinely required before conservative management of uncomplicated SUI; indicated when surgery is considered or diagnosis uncertain. Urodynamics is correct before surgical planning (NICE NG123).

explanation.summary: Urinary incontinence is classified as stress (leakage on cough/sneeze/exertion), urge (preceded by urgency, part of overactive bladder), mixed, or overflow (chronic retention with leakage, raised PVR). Initial assessment includes history, bladder diary, urinalysis, PVR, and cough stress test. First-line management for stress and mixed UI is conservative: pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT) for at least 3 months, weight loss, smoking cessation, and treatment of constipation. Failure of conservative therapy escalates to mid-urethral sling for stress UI or antimuscarinics/beta-3 agonists for urge UI.
key_points: Stress UI: leak with cough/sneeze, no urgency
Urge UI: leak preceded by urgency, often nocturia and frequency
Overflow UI: raised PVR, chronic retention
PFMT first-line for at least 3 months supervised
Antimuscarinics/mirabegron for urge; sling for stress that fails conservative Rx
explanation.pearls: If you cannot reproduce leakage with a cough stress test and the post-void residual is high, you are not dealing with stress incontinence - rethink the diagnosis before recommending a sling.

subtopic_detail: Urinary incontinence - stress vs urge distinction and initial management

stem:
A 68-year-old G4P4 woman presents with 6 months of a vaginal bulge worse at end of day. She also has bothersome stress urinary incontinence on coughing/sneezing and needs to splint to void fully. No faecal incontinence, no dyspareunia. Sexually active with husband, BMI 27, hypertension on perindopril, no other comorbidities. POP-Q: Ba 0, C -2, Bp -3 (stage 2 anterior compartment).

Post-void residual 80 mL. Urodynamic studies confirm urodynamic stress incontinence with no detrusor overactivity and good bladder capacity. She wants the option with the best evidence for both prolapse and urinary symptoms; she is prepared to consider surgery but would also consider a pessary.

data_table.Examination: POP-Q: Ba = 0 (anterior wall at hymen on Valsalva), C = -2, Bp = -3. Stage 2 anterior compartment prolapse.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management?

options.text: Trial vaginal ring pessary and supervised pelvic floor physiotherapy, with reassessment of urinary symptoms after prolapse reduction
options.rationale: Conservative management (pessary + pelvic floor physiotherapy) is first-line for symptomatic POP including in women with concurrent stress incontinence. Pessary may unmask or improve SUI; reassessment after reduction guides whether surgery for incontinence is needed (RANZCOG; IUGA).

options.text: Anterior colporrhaphy with concomitant retropubic midurethral sling
options.rationale: Mesh midurethral sling implantation has been restricted by TGA in Australia following safety concerns; combined surgery is not first-line. Mesh sling is correct only after conservative failure with formal consent and TGA-compliant device choice (RANZCOG; TGA).

options.text: Sacrocolpopexy with synthetic mesh under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Abdominal mesh sacrocolpopexy is reserved for apical/post-hysterectomy prolapse, not first-line for stage-2 anterior compartment; restricted in Australia. Sacrocolpopexy is correct for refractory apical prolapse after conservative failure (TGA mesh).

options.text: Indwelling urinary catheter to manage the elevated post-void residual long-term
options.rationale: PVR of 80 mL is mildly raised and likely improves with prolapse reduction; long-term catheter risks infection. IDC is correct only with refractory retention not improved by other measures (RANZCOG).

options.text: Topical oestrogen as monotherapy with no other intervention
options.rationale: Vaginal oestrogen is an adjunct to pessary tolerance but does not treat prolapse or SUI alone. Topical oestrogen is correct as adjunct to pessary use or for atrophic symptoms (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: POP-Q stages prolapse by the most distal point relative to the hymen: stage 0 no prolapse; stage 1 most distal point >1 cm above hymen; stage 2 within 1 cm of hymen; stage 3 >1 cm beyond hymen but not complete eversion; stage 4 complete eversion. Conservative management (pessary, pelvic floor physiotherapy, topical oestrogen if postmenopausal) is first-line for symptomatic prolapse, particularly in women who wish to avoid surgery or have surgical contraindications. Surgical options include native tissue anterior/posterior repair, sacrocolpopexy for apical defect, and colpocleisis in non-sexually-active women with comorbidities.
key_points: POP-Q stage 2 = leading point within 1 cm of hymen (-1 to +1)
Conservative: pessary + PFMT + topical oestrogen if postmenopausal
Pessary types: ring (mild-moderate), Gellhorn (advanced), cube (refractory)
Sacrocolpopexy = gold standard apical repair
Mesh use restricted in Australia following TGA review; native tissue preferred
explanation.pearls: Stage 2 anterior wall prolapse with urinary symptoms often masks occult stress incontinence; reducing the prolapse with a pessary can unmask leakage that the patient did not previously notice.

subtopic_detail: Pelvic organ prolapse - conservative management of symptomatic stage 2 cystocele

stem:
A 32-year-old G0 presents with 14 months of constant pelvic pain rated 5/10 at baseline and 8/10 cyclically. The pain has both cyclical features (worse in the 4 days prior to and during her period) and non-cyclical features (dull background ache present every day for the last 8 months). She reports dyspareunia on deep penetration, dyschezia during menses, and increased urinary frequency without dysuria or haematuria.

She has been trying to conceive for 9 months without success. There is no fever, no postmenopausal bleeding, and no haematuria. Her mother had endometriosis diagnosed at laparoscopy. On bimanual examination there is uterosacral nodularity and a fixed retroverted uterus with cervical excitation. A pelvic ultrasound 2 months ago showed a 3 cm left ovarian endometrioma with ground-glass appearance and normal uterine cavity. STI screen today is pending.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, T 36.6 degC, BMI 23.
data_table.Examination: Uterosacral nodularity. Fixed retroverted uterus. Cervical excitation. No palpable adnexal mass on bimanual.
data_table.Investigations: TVUS: 3 cm left ovarian endometrioma. Normal uterine cavity. STI screen pending. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure that endometriosis is confirmed on ultrasound and commence the COCP continuously without further investigation
options.rationale: Although picture is consistent with endometriosis, fertility goals, deep endometriosis on examination, and pending STI screen require integrated specialist assessment; COCP suppresses fertility in someone trying to conceive. COCP is correct in endometriosis when not actively conceiving (RANZCOG Endometriosis Statement; ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).

options.text: Insert a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine device now and review her symptoms in 6 months
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is effective for endometriosis pain but is contraceptive and inappropriate while actively trying to conceive. LNG-IUS is correct in endometriosis pain management when fertility not desired (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).

options.text: Start a GnRH agonist with add-back tibolone for 6 months and reassess symptoms
options.rationale: GnRH agonists are second-line for refractory endometriosis; contraceptive and would delay fertility. GnRH agonists are correct in refractory endometriosis after first-line failure when fertility not immediate goal (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).

options.text: Proceed directly to diagnostic laparoscopy without prior imaging or specialist multidisciplinary review at all
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is no longer required to diagnose endometriosis when imaging and clinical features are characteristic; preoperative MRI for deep disease mapping and multidisciplinary planning improves outcomes and protects fertility. Direct laparoscopy is correct only when imaging is inconclusive after MRI mapping (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).

options.text: Refer to a multidisciplinary endometriosis service, arrange MRI for deep infiltrating disease, and address fertility
options.rationale: Suspected deep infiltrating endometriosis (uterosacral nodularity, dyschezia, fixed retroversion) with an endometrioma and subfertility warrants MRI mapping and multidisciplinary specialist input combining gynaecology, fertility, pelvic pain physiotherapy and psychology; the Australian National Action Plan for Endometriosis recommends this pathway (RANZCOG Endometriosis Statement; National Action Plan for Endometriosis; Pelvic Pain Foundation Australia).

explanation.summary: Chronic pelvic pain workup screens for red flags (postmenopausal bleeding, weight loss, PR bleeding, haematuria, pelvic mass), addresses the bladder, bowel, musculoskeletal, neurological and psychosocial dimensions, and identifies treatable gynaecological pathology (endometriosis, adenomyosis, fibroids, PID, ovarian cyst). Endometriosis often has clinical and imaging features that are diagnostic without laparoscopy. Deep infiltrating endometriosis (uterosacral, bowel, bladder, ureteric involvement) requires MRI mapping and multidisciplinary surgical planning. Management is biopsychosocial: medical (NSAID, COCP, progestogen, LNG-IUS, GnRH analogues), surgical (excision/ablation, fertility-preserving), pain-focused (CBT, physiotherapy, mindfulness), and fertility-specific (IVF if needed).
key_points: CPP red flags: PMB, weight loss, PR bleeding, haematuria, fever, mass
Endometriosis no longer requires laparoscopy for diagnosis if imaging/clinical features are characteristic
Deep endometriosis suspected: MRI mapping + multidisciplinary review
LNG-IUS, COCP, progestogens are first-line medical Rx (contraceptive)
Fertility goals dictate therapy choice; consider IVF early in deep disease
explanation.pearls: Uterosacral nodularity and a fixed retroverted uterus on bimanual examination are physical signs of deep infiltrating endometriosis - imaging may underestimate disease, so multidisciplinary review precedes any surgical decision.

subtopic_detail: Chronic pelvic pain - red flag identification and biopsychosocial workup

stem:
A 29-year-old G1P1 presents for preconception counselling. Her first child was born 18 months ago at 39 weeks via uncomplicated vaginal birth weighing 3450 g. She takes sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for generalised tonic-clonic epilepsy (last seizure 4 years ago) and is co-managed by neurology. She is otherwise well. She has no personal or family history of neural tube defects, no diabetes, no malabsorption, and a BMI of 24.

She is up to date with her cervical screening (HPV negative 8 months ago) and her booking bloods from her last pregnancy showed she was rubella immune and varicella immune. She smokes 5 cigarettes a day, drinks 4 standard drinks twice a week on weekends, and does not use any other supplements. She would like to start trying for a second child in 3 months and asks what she should do to prepare.

data_table.Investigations: Rubella IgG immune (last pregnancy). Varicella IgG immune. Cervical screen HPV negative 8 months ago. HbA1c 5.2% (33 mmol/mol).

lead_in: Which is the most important pre-conception intervention?

options.text: Folic acid 0.5 mg orally daily for 3 months pre-conception and the first trimester only
options.rationale: Standard 0.5 mg is for low-risk women; sodium valproate is major teratogen requiring 5 mg daily. Low-dose folate is correct for women without teratogenic medications, prior NTD, diabetes, malabsorption, or BMI >30 (RANZCOG; NHMRC).

options.text: Iodine 150 microg daily plus vitamin D level testing only without any medication change overall
options.rationale: Iodine 150 microg/day is recommended pre-pregnancy but does not address dominant teratogenic risk from sodium valproate. Iodine alone is correct as standard pre-conception supplement, but inadequate as single intervention here (RANZCOG; NHMRC).

options.text: Reassure her that no change to current AED therapy is needed because she is presently seizure-free
options.rationale: Seizure control does not mitigate teratogenic risk of valproate; the drug itself is the harm irrespective of seizure control. No-change is correct only when alternative AEDs have failed and informed decision is documented (RANZCOG; AMH).

options.text: Administer MMR and varicella booster immunisations now before any pregnancy attempt is made by this patient
options.rationale: She is already immune to rubella and varicella; live vaccines not indicated and contraindicated within 28 days of attempting conception. MMR/varicella is correct only with sub-protective titres and 4-week conception delay (Australian Immunisation Handbook).

options.text: Refer to neurology to switch to a safer anti-seizure medication and prescribe folic acid 5 mg daily
options.rationale: Sodium valproate carries the highest teratogenic risk of any anti-seizure medication (10% major congenital malformation rate, neurodevelopmental effects in 30 to 40%); switching to a safer agent (e.g. lamotrigine or levetiracetam) before conception is mandatory and high-dose folate 5 mg is indicated because of the elevated neural tube defect risk (RANZCOG; NHMRC; AMH).

explanation.summary: Pre-conception counselling covers folate (0.5 mg/day low-risk; 5 mg/day high-risk: previous NTD, diabetes, anti-epileptic drugs, malabsorption, family history NTD, obesity BMI >30 in some guidelines), iodine 150 microg/day, rubella and varicella serology with vaccination if non-immune (4 weeks before conception), cervical screening, STI screening, optimisation of chronic disease (diabetes, hypertension, thyroid, epilepsy), medication review for teratogens (valproate, ACE inhibitors, warfarin, retinoids, methotrexate), smoking and alcohol cessation, weight optimisation, mental health, and partner factors.
key_points: Folate low-risk 0.5 mg; high-risk 5 mg from 1 to 3 months pre-conception through 12 weeks
Iodine 150 microg/day pre-conception and throughout pregnancy and lactation
Live vaccines (MMR, varicella) at least 4 weeks before conception attempts
Sodium valproate is the most teratogenic AED; switch pre-conception
Other teratogens: ACEi/ARB, warfarin, isotretinoin, methotrexate, mycophenolate
explanation.pearls: If a woman of reproductive age is taking sodium valproate, the bigger intervention is changing the drug, not adding folate; high-dose folate is mitigation, not protection.

stem:
A 33-year-old G3P0 presents after her third consecutive first-trimester miscarriage. The first was a missed miscarriage at 9 weeks managed expectantly, the second was a complete miscarriage at 8 weeks managed at home, and the third was a missed miscarriage at 10+2 managed with misoprostol 4 weeks ago. She conceives easily within 1 to 2 cycles each time and has regular 28-day ovulatory cycles.

She has no personal history of venous thromboembolism, deep vein thrombosis, stroke, or pulmonary embolism, no symptoms of systemic lupus erythematosus (no malar rash, no arthralgia), no autoimmune disease, no fertility treatment, and no live births. She has never smoked, drinks one standard drink per week, has a BMI of 27, and is otherwise well. There is no family history of recurrent miscarriage, balanced translocation, congenital malformation, or genetic disease, and no consanguinity.

A transvaginal ultrasound shows a normal uterine cavity, both ovaries normal, and no fibroids; antral follicle count is normal. Karyotype of products of conception from the third miscarriage was 46,XX (euploid). TSH is 1.8 mIU/L and HbA1c is 5.4% (35 mmol/mol). She asks what should be tested before her next pregnancy.

data_table.Investigations: TVUS: normal uterine cavity, no fibroids, both ovaries normal. POC karyotype third miscarriage 46,XX (euploid). TSH 1.8 mIU/L. HbA1c 5.4% (35 mmol/mol).

lead_in: Which is the most important next investigation?

options.text: Parental peripheral blood karyotype testing as the very first single investigation in this workup
options.rationale: Parental karyotype is not routinely first-line; reserved for couples with euploid first-trimester loss plus additional features (advanced maternal age, family history) or structural rearrangement on products. Karyotype is correct with structural rearrangement on POC or multiple losses with concerning family history (ESHRE RPL 2023; RANZCOG).

options.text: Inherited thrombophilia screen including factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene, protein C, protein S, antithrombin
options.rationale: Inherited thrombophilia testing is not recommended in recurrent miscarriage outside research because results do not change management. Thrombophilia testing is correct only when there is a separate VTE indication, not for RPL alone (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

options.text: Diagnostic hysteroscopy with directed biopsies regardless of any prior pelvic ultrasound findings reported recently
options.rationale: Hysteroscopy/SIS is reserved for cases where US is equivocal or suggests cavity abnormality; her TVUS shows a normal cavity. Hysteroscopy is correct when US is suspicious for intrauterine pathology (ESHRE).

options.text: Lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, and anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-1 antibodies repeated on two occasions 12 weeks apart
options.rationale: After 3 consecutive miscarriages (or 2 by RANZCOG criteria), antiphospholipid syndrome screening is the highest yield test that has direct therapeutic implications (aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH in next pregnancy if positive); diagnosis requires laboratory criteria on two occasions at least 12 weeks apart (RANZCOG Recurrent Miscarriage; ESHRE RPL 2023).

options.text: Peripheral blood natural killer cell assay as the very first single investigation here
options.rationale: NK cell testing is not validated for RPL and is explicitly not recommended by ESHRE and RANZCOG. NK testing is essentially never correct in routine RPL workup (ESHRE RPL 2023).

explanation.summary: Recurrent pregnancy loss is defined by RANZCOG as 2 or more consecutive miscarriages and by some international bodies as 3 or more. Workup with evidence-based yield includes APS screen (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, anti-beta-2-GP-I on two occasions 12 weeks apart), thyroid function and TPO antibodies, HbA1c, parental karyotype if products show structural abnormality, and pelvic imaging to exclude uterine anomaly. APS is the only treatable cause with clear therapeutic benefit: low-dose aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH improves live birth rates.
key_points: RANZCOG RPL definition: >=2 consecutive miscarriages
APS labs: LA, aCL, anti-beta-2-GP-I, repeated 12 weeks apart
APS treatment: aspirin 100 mg + prophylactic LMWH in pregnancy
Thyroid (TSH, TPO) and HbA1c also worth screening
Inherited thrombophilia and NK cell tests are NOT recommended
explanation.pearls: Half of all recurrent miscarriages remain unexplained even after thorough workup; the most important counselling point is that an unexplained workup still predicts a 60 to 75% chance of subsequent live birth.

subtopic_detail: Recurrent pregnancy loss - antiphospholipid syndrome workup

stem:
A 30-year-old G0 and her 32-year-old male partner present after 13 months of trying to conceive with regular unprotected intercourse 2 to 3 times per week, including timed intercourse around predicted ovulation in the last 4 cycles. She has regular 29-day cycles with predictable ovulatory symptoms (mid-cycle mucus change and mittelschmerz). She has no dysmenorrhoea, no dyspareunia, and was up to date with cervical screening 6 months ago (HPV negative).

BMI 24, non-smoker, no alcohol, no caffeine excess. She takes folic acid 0.5 mg daily and iodine 150 microg daily. Her partner is well, BMI 26, non-smoker, no known fertility issues, no previous children, takes no medications, drinks 6 standard drinks per week, and his work as an electrician does not involve heat exposure. She is concerned and asks what investigations should be done now.

data_table.Investigations: Cervical screen HPV negative 6 months ago. Beta-hCG negative.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial set of investigations?

options.text: Semen analysis, day-21 progesterone, TSH and transvaginal ultrasound; reserve HSG for after initial workup
options.rationale: Infertility is defined as 12 months of unprotected intercourse without conception in women under 35 (6 months if 35 or over). First-line investigations are semen analysis (assesses 40 to 50% of contributory causes), confirmation of ovulation (mid-luteal progesterone), thyroid function, and pelvic ultrasound. Tubal patency assessment (HSG or HyCoSy) is performed after initial workup if no abnormality is found (RANZCOG Infertility; NICE CG156).

options.text: Refer immediately for IVF without any further investigation of the couple
options.rationale: IVF is indicated after a defined cause is identified or following empirical workup; jumping to IVF without identifying treatable causes is inappropriate and ignores potential male factor. IVF is correct after workup identifies indications (RANZCOG).

options.text: Continue trying for another 12 months before any investigation of the couple
options.rationale: She is over 12 months of regular unprotected intercourse and meets the definition of infertility; delaying investigation reduces chances of timely treatment. Continued attempts are correct only if duration is below the 12 month (or 6 month if >=35) threshold (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).

options.text: Hysterosalpingogram and diagnostic laparoscopy as the first-line investigations of the couple
options.rationale: Tubal patency assessment is part of workup but follows initial investigations; laparoscopy as first-line is invasive and unjustified. HSG/laparoscopy is correct after initial workup is unrevealing (RANZCOG; NICE CG156).

options.text: AMH for ovarian reserve only and reassure her she can wait given young age
options.rationale: AMH is part of broader workup but does not predict spontaneous conception; reassurance without male factor or tubal workup misses 50% of contributory causes. AMH-only is correct as adjunctive ovarian reserve assessment, never as sole investigation (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

explanation.summary: Infertility is defined as 12 months of unprotected intercourse without conception in women under 35, or 6 months at age 35 or older, or earlier if there is a known cause. Initial workup is parallel investigation of both partners: semen analysis (according to WHO criteria), ovulation assessment (mid-luteal progesterone, cycle history, ultrasound follicle tracking), ovarian reserve markers (AMH, antral follicle count, FSH on day 2 to 5), thyroid, prolactin, and pelvic ultrasound. Tubal patency assessment (HyCoSy or HSG) follows. Laparoscopy is reserved for suspected endometriosis or adhesive disease.
key_points: <35: investigate after 12 months; >=35: investigate after 6 months
Male factor accounts for 40 to 50% - always do semen analysis
Mid-luteal (day 21 in 28-day cycle) progesterone >25 nmol/L = ovulation
Tubal patency: HyCoSy preferred (no radiation) or HSG
AMH for ovarian reserve, not as a predictor of spontaneous conception
explanation.pearls: The most common omission in infertility workup is the semen analysis - both partners must be investigated in parallel from the outset.

subtopic_detail: Infertility workup in a couple under 35

stem:
A 15-year-old presents with her mother because of worsening period pain and bleeding. Menarche was at 12; cycles are regular at 28 days but have become increasingly painful since age 14, now causing her to miss 3 to 5 school days per month. Pain begins 24 hours before bleeding, peaks on day 2, and is associated with nausea, vomiting, and inability to get out of bed.

She soaks through a fully saturated super pad every 90 minutes on days 1 and 2 and reports passing 5-cent-piece-sized clots. She has tried paracetamol 1 g qid and ibuprofen 400 mg tds with only partial relief. Her mother had endometriosis diagnosed at laparoscopy at age 22 and a paternal aunt has known endometriosis. There is no dyspareunia (she is not sexually active), no dyschezia, no haematuria, and no postcoital bleeding.

She is no longer attending netball training and has begun to refuse social outings and sleepovers during her periods, and her mother reports she has been increasingly tearful and withdrawn in the days leading up to bleeding. On abdominal examination there is mild suprapubic tenderness; she declines an internal examination. A pregnancy test is negative.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 108/64 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, T 36.5 degC, BMI 22.
data_table.Examination: Mild suprapubic tenderness. Declines internal examination.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative. FBC: Hb 116 g/L, MCV 82 fL, ferritin 18 microg/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Naproxen 500 mg bd (day -1 to 3), continuous COCP and pelvic ultrasound; refer if no improvement
options.rationale: Adolescent dysmenorrhoea with severe functional impact, heavy bleeding, family history of endometriosis, and inadequate response to first-line analgesia warrants empirical scheduled NSAID plus continuous (cycle-suppressing) COCP, transabdominal pelvic ultrasound (transvaginal not appropriate in a non-sexually-active adolescent), and specialist referral if no improvement at 3 months; diagnosis does not require laparoscopy in adolescents (RANZCOG Endometriosis; Pelvic Pain Foundation Australia; ESHRE).

options.text: Reassure her that severe period pain in adolescence is normal and continue her current analgesia regimen long-term
options.rationale: Missing 3-5 school days/month, social withdrawal, and inadequate response to first-line analgesia is NOT normal and reflects high probability of endometriosis or other secondary cause. Reassurance is correct for mild primary dysmenorrhoea responsive to NSAIDs (Pelvic Pain Foundation Australia; RANZCOG).

options.text: Proceed directly to diagnostic laparoscopy without trialling any medical management as the first step
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is not first-line in adolescents; empirical medical therapy is trialled first. Laparoscopy is correct after failed 3-6 month trial of medical management or suspected deep disease (RANZCOG; ESHRE).

options.text: Commence a GnRH agonist with hormone add-back therapy as the next immediate step in management
options.rationale: GnRH agonists are reserved for refractory endometriosis after first-line hormonal failure; long-term use in adolescents has bone density concerns. GnRH agonists are correct in refractory disease with bone protection (ESHRE).

options.text: Insert a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system without trialling any prior medical therapy first
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is effective but is second-line after COCP trial in non-sexually-active adolescent; insertion may require GA. LNG-IUS is correct after COCP failure or when COCP contraindicated (Family Planning Australia; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Adolescent endometriosis is under-recognised; diagnostic delay averages 7 to 12 years. School avoidance, heavy menstrual bleeding, severe dysmenorrhoea unresponsive to first-line analgesia, and a family history are red flags. Empirical management combines scheduled NSAID, continuous (suppressive) COCP to eliminate menstruation, transabdominal pelvic ultrasound, and early specialist referral. Transvaginal ultrasound is generally not appropriate in non-sexually-active adolescents. LNG-IUS is a second-line option and may require GA for insertion. GnRH agonists are reserved for refractory disease and require add-back therapy. Laparoscopy is not required to diagnose endometriosis in adolescents when the clinical picture is consistent.
key_points: Diagnostic delay in endometriosis averages 7 to 12 years
Adolescent red flags: school absence, family hx, refractory to first-line
Empirical Rx: scheduled NSAID + continuous COCP; TA ultrasound
TVUS inappropriate in non-sexually-active adolescent
LNG-IUS effective but second-line in adolescents; consider GA for insertion
explanation.pearls: If a 15-year-old is missing school every month for period pain, do not normalise it - empirical treatment plus follow-up is the standard of care, and the family history doubles the prior probability of endometriosis.

subtopic_detail: Adolescent endometriosis - high index of suspicion and empirical management

stem:
A 14-year-old presents to the emergency department with a 4-hour history of sudden severe left iliac fossa pain. The pain began abruptly while she was playing netball, is sharp, constant, radiates to the left flank, and has been associated with vomiting four times in the last 2 hours. She had two previous brief episodes of less severe left-sided pain in the last 6 months, each lasting 30 minutes and resolving spontaneously.

Menarche was at 13; her last menstrual period was 12 days ago and was normal. She has never been sexually active. There is no fever, no urinary symptoms, and no chronic illness. On examination she is in obvious distress, BP 110/68, HR 116, T 37.0, with marked left iliac fossa tenderness and guarding, and a tender palpable mass. Abdomen is otherwise soft.

Beta-hCG is negative. White cell count is 13.4 x 10^9/L, CRP 18 mg/L. A transabdominal pelvic ultrasound with Doppler shows a 7 cm complex left adnexal mass with peripherally displaced follicles, a hyperechoic whirlpool sign at the suspected pedicle, and absent venous flow with reduced arterial flow within the left ovary. The right ovary is normal. There is no free fluid.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 110/68 mmHg, HR 116 bpm, T 37.0 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Marked left iliac fossa tenderness with guarding. Tender left adnexal mass palpable.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG negative. WCC 13.4 x 10^9/L. CRP 18 mg/L. TVUS Doppler: 7 cm complex left adnexal mass, peripherally displaced follicles, whirlpool sign, absent venous flow, reduced arterial flow. Right ovary normal. No free fluid.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for observation and analgesia and repeat the ultrasound in 12 hours
options.rationale: Ovarian torsion is a time-critical surgical emergency; every hour of delay increases ovarian necrosis and reduces salvage rate. Observation is correct only with low clinical suspicion and reassuring imaging (RANZCOG; KEMH).

options.text: Pelvic MRI to confirm diagnosis before any surgical decision is made
options.rationale: MRI adds delay without improving on Doppler US findings that are already characteristic; diagnosis is clinical and US-based warranting immediate surgery. MRI is correct only when US is non-diagnostic and clinical suspicion is moderate (RANZCOG).

options.text: Emergency laparoscopy for detorsion and ovarian preservation, with oophoropexy considered intraoperatively
options.rationale: Whirlpool sign, peripherally displaced follicles and absent venous flow on Doppler in an adolescent with characteristic clinical features is ovarian torsion. The standard of care is emergency laparoscopy with detorsion regardless of macroscopic appearance (ovaries that look black often recover function); oophorectomy is reserved for necrotic non-viable tissue or malignancy. Oophoropexy is considered to reduce recurrence (RANZCOG Ovarian Cysts; ESHRE; Royal Women's Hospital Clinical Guidelines).

options.text: Emergency laparoscopy with planned left salpingo-oophorectomy because the ovary appears non-viable on Doppler
options.rationale: Doppler signs do not reliably predict viability; ovaries appearing black often recover function after detorsion. Oophorectomy in adolescent should be reserved for confirmed necrosis or malignancy. Salpingo-oophorectomy is correct only with intra-operative confirmation of non-viability or malignancy (RANZCOG; Royal Women's Hospital).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and admit for suspected pelvic inflammatory disease
options.rationale: She has never been sexually active and imaging is diagnostic of torsion; antibiotics do not treat torsion and delay surgery. Antibiotics are correct in confirmed PID, never in torsion (Australian STI Management Guidelines; RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency. Clinical features are sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, often with vomiting, intermittent prior episodes, and a palpable adnexal mass. Doppler ultrasound findings of whirlpool sign, peripherally displaced follicles, and absent venous flow with reduced arterial flow support the diagnosis, but normal Doppler does NOT exclude torsion (dual ovarian blood supply can preserve arterial flow). Management is emergency laparoscopy with detorsion and ovarian preservation; oophorectomy is reserved for confirmed necrosis or malignancy. Oophoropexy is considered to reduce recurrence, particularly when no ovarian mass is found or in cases with redundant ligaments. Outcomes correlate with time to surgery.
key_points: Sudden severe unilateral pain + vomiting + adnexal mass = torsion until excluded
Doppler whirlpool sign, peripheral follicles, absent venous flow = highly suggestive
Normal Doppler does NOT exclude torsion (dual blood supply)
Detorsion + ovarian preservation is the standard, even if the ovary looks ischaemic
Oophoropexy reduces recurrence, especially when no mass is present
explanation.pearls: Black does not equal dead - detorsed ischaemic-looking ovaries regularly recover function in adolescents, and the decision to remove an ovary in a 14-year-old should be reserved for clear necrosis or proven malignancy.

subtopic_detail: Ovarian torsion in an adolescent - urgent surgical management
